g.data-narration: The target language is the programming language that a compiler translates source code into, usually assembly or machine code.
g.text: The programming language that the compiler translates source code into.
Typically Assembly Language or Machine Code for a specific target platform.

g.data-narration: The target language sits between source code and machine code in the compilation pipeline, serving as an intermediate representation.
Several factors influence target language selection: platform requirements, performance needs, code complexity, and implementation costs.
A good target language should be specific to the platform, simple to understand, flexible for programming styles, and scalable.
Assembly language is a low-level target language specific to a platform architecture, translated directly into machine code.
g.text: Low-level language close to machine code.

g.data-narration: High-level target languages are more abstract than assembly, offering flexibility while remaining closer to machine code than source languages.
g.text: More complex and flexible than assembly.

g.data-narration: The x86 assembly language is a real-world example, specific to x86 architecture and directly translatable to machine code.
Understanding platform, architecture, machine code, assembly language, and high-level language as target language concepts.
Addressing modes determine how operands are specified: immediate, direct, indirect, indexed, and relative addressing.
RISC uses simple instructions with registers while CISC has complex instructions - each affects code generation differently.
g.text: Simple instructions • Load/Store architecture • Many registers • Fixed length
Complex instructions • Memory-to-memory • Few registers • Variable length

g.data-narration: The target machine model includes register sets, memory organization, and instruction formats essential for code generation.
In this chapter we explored target language definition, types (assembly and high-level), characteristics, and real-world examples like x86.
g.text: • Target language is the output of compiler - usually Assembly or Machine Code
• Two types: Low-level Assembly (platform-specific) vs High-level (abstract IR)
• Key factors: Platform, Performance, Complexity, Cost

g.data-narration: Remember: Target language bridges source and machine code. It's platform-dependent, low-level, and critical for code generation in compilers.
g.text: Target language = Bridge between Source Code and Machine Code
Platform-independent intermediate representation | Low-level, close to machine | Essential for code generation
